comment.body:
hey all, I am starting to get behind everything Alma writes and to really understand everything (that is the goal). Currently I am having a bit of a problem with GEX. I now understand that: 
- positive GEX = market makers sell when the price moves up, they buy when the price moves down (essentially creating support and resistance)
- negative GEX = market makers sell when the price moves down, they buy when the price moves up (essentially amplifying volatility and creating even larger price swings)
- High GEX tends to create a pinning effect. 

Now I am reading this post here and I don't get the Case Study #3. The price acts as magnet (because large gamma position) but why would we actually expect the underlying to go to these levels? Especially if it is positive GEX (which tells us, that market makers sell when the price goes up). 

An explanation would be very much appreciated!

- High GEX tends to create a pinning effect. 

Now I am reading this post here and I don't get the Case Study #3. The price acts as magnet (because large gamma position) but why would we actually expect the underlying to go to these levels? Especially if it is positive GEX (which tells us, that market makers sell when the price goes up). 

An explanation would be very much appreciated!

comment.body:
there is a difference between net and gross exposures.
lets look at the screenshot of the photo you attached of my drawing: that positive speed profile is net positive, as you can see gamma can fluctuate across the strikes. I made that this way purposely exactly to illustrate this.
so the net chamr exposure can be negative, but it fluctuates. and the net charm exposure is also a wave. 
You can see on the graph: as we are heading into the center of the fly pattern, both the vanna curve and charm curve approaches zero, so the exposure is decreasin or increasing ... it fluctuates. 

There is no exact answer where they change... we have to look at the positioning, and also note that we will never see regular curves, but noise ones
comment.raw_body:
there is a difference between net and gross exposures.
lets look at the screenshot of the photo you attached of my drawing: that positive speed profile is net positive, as you can see gamma can fluctuate across the strikes. I made that this way purposely exactly to illustrate this.
so the net chamr exposure can be negative, but it fluctuates. and the net charm exposure is also a wave. 
You can see on the graph: as we are heading into the center of the fly pattern, both the vanna curve and charm curve approaches zero, so the exposure is decreasin or increasing ... it fluctuates. 

There is no exact answer where they change... we have to look at the positioning, and also note that we will never see regular curves, but noise ones

comment.body:
gamma is not a bout creating support or resistance lvls... the primal effect is to swallow or expand volatility... add or decrease liquidity.
and SOMETIMES they act as support/resistance lvls... sometimes... but it depends on many more things... read my OD chat post.

the effect that creates the magnetic effect is positive chamr convexisty in a MM short butterfly pattern. 
you have negative charm below the price and positive charm above it.
as expo comes, gamma increases, the speed profile becomes steeper in the fly pattern, and the charm effect increases.
negative charm below the high gamma strike makes dealers shorter delta, triggering buyback flow... but above it is positive charm that makes them longer delta, forcing them to sell futures.
the momentum gets locked up to that zone, and gamma pins it at the end, no espcape as high gamma counter- adjusments needed for algos whenever the price wants to go up or down.
But only if vol is low
